10: David's response to Abishai's request to execute Shimei for his cursing is the Succession Narrative's most counterintuitive theological statement: mah lî wālāḵem bᵉnê ṣᵉrûyāh kî yᵉqallēl waYHWH ʾāmar lô qallēl ʾet dāwiḏ — 'What have I to do with you, you sons of Zeruiah? Let him curse, for YHWH has said to him, “Curse David.”' David's interpretation of the unjust curse as YHWH's instrument (YHWH ʾāmar lô qallēl) is not fatalism but covenantal theology: the same theological logic by which Joseph interpreted his brothers' betrayal (Genesis 50:20), by which Job refused to curse God in suffering (Job 1:21), and by which Peter interprets the crucifixion (Acts 2:23: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God'). The suffering king who refuses to strike down his curser exercises the same patience that 1 Peter 2:23 ascribes to Christ: 'when he suffered, he did not threaten.' David under Shimei's curse is an enacted type of the NT theology of patiently bearing reproach as YHWH's purposeful instrument.
1: Ve-David avar me'at me-ha-rosh ve-hinneh Tziva na'ar Mephiboshet liqra'to — Ziba's meeting with David at the summit of the Mount of Olives. Me'at me-ha-rosh (a little beyond the summit) places the encounter just after the Mount of Olives worship site. The provisions: two saddled donkeys (chamor sheni chavushim), two hundred loaves of bread (lechem matayim), a hundred clusters of raisins (tzemidei tzimmuqim), a hundred summer fruits (qayitz), and a skin of wine — a complete commissariat provision.
2: Vayomer ha-melekh el-Tziva: mah-elleh lakh — David's inquiry about the purpose of the provisions. Ziba maps each provision to a recipient group: the donkeys for the royal household to ride, the bread and summer fruit for the young men (ne'arim), the wine for the faint (ya'ef ba-midbar, the exhausted in the wilderness). The mapping demonstrates logistical thinking and genuine usefulness — making Ziba's concurrent slander more effective by the contrast of his apparent care.
3: Va-yomer ha-melekh: ve-ayeh ben-adonekha — David's question about Mephibosheth. Ziba's accusation: yashav bi-Yerushalaim ki amar: ha-yom yashivu li beit Yisra'el et-mamlekhut avi — “he remains in Jerusalem, for he said, Today the house of Israel will restore to me the kingdom of my father.” Yashivu li (they will restore to me) uses the Hiphil of shuv — the politically charged vocabulary of restoration. Whether Mephibosheth said this (cf. 2 Samuel 19:24–30) cannot be determined; Ziba's unverified report is the only evidence David hears before judging.
4: Vayomer ha-melekh le-Tziva: hinneh lekha kol asher li-Mephiboshet — David's immediate judgment: everything that belongs to Mephibosheth is Ziba's. Hinneh lekha (behold, to you) is a royal grant formula — immediate and categorical. The decision made under duress without hearing Mephibosheth will haunt David; in 19:29 he splits the property — a compromise that implicitly acknowledges the rashness of the original judgment.
5: U-va David ad-Bachurim ve-hinneh mi-sham yotze ish mi-mishpachat beit-Sha'ul u-shemo Shim'i ben-Gera yotze yatzo u-meqallel — Shimei (Shim'i ben-Gera) coming out cursing (meqallel, Piel participle of qalal, to curse, diminish). The Piel of qalal was the formal vocabulary of cursing — calling down diminishment and contempt on the target. Mi-mishpachat beit-Sha'ul identifies the tribal and dynastic motive: Benjaminite loyalty to the Saulide house.
6: Va-yeqallel va-yishlakh avanim el-David ve-el kol-avdei ha-melekh David — throwing stones (shalakh avanim) at David and all his servants. Stone-throwing was a calculated insult and physical assault. Ve-khol-ha-am ve-khol-ha-gibborim mi-ymino u-mi-smolo — all the people and all the mighty men (gibborim) flanking David — the elite warriors who could have killed Shimei instantly, yet David commands restraint. The power asymmetry is deliberate.
7: Ve-kho amar Shim'i be-qaleloh: tze tze ish ha-damim ve-ish ha-beliya'al — tze tze is the intensive expulsion formula — a doubled imperative of yatza (go out). Ish ha-damim (man of blood/bloodshed) — the construct phrase of bloodguilt. Ish ha-beliya'al (man of worthlessness, beliya'al = without value/profit) — the strongest social condemnation combining criminal charge with moral nullity.
8: Heshiv alekha YHWH kol-damei beit Sha'ul asher malakhta tachtav — Shimei's theological indictment: YHWH has returned (heshiv, Hiphil of shuv) upon David all the blood of Saul's house for which David ruled in his place. Va-yitten YHWH et-ha-mamlakhah be-yad Avshalom binekha — YHWH has given the kingdom into Absalom's hand. Shimei's theology of direct divine retribution — the coup as YHWH's punishment for David's bloodguilt — is not endorsed by the narrator but is also not directly refuted by David.
9: Vayomer Avishai ben-Tzeruyah el-ha-melekh: lammah yeqallel ha-kelev ha-met ha-zeh et-adoni ha-melekh — Abishai's characterization of Shimei as ha-kelev ha-met ha-zeh (this dead dog). Kelev met (dead dog) is the lowest social category in Hebrew social vocabulary — combining the uncleanness of the dog with the powerlessness of death. The term appears in 1 Samuel 24:15 (David's self-deprecation) and 2 Samuel 9:8 (Mephibosheth's self-deprecation) — here deployed as the maximum insult for Shimei.
11: Vayomer David el-Avishai ve-el kol-avadav: hinneh beni asher yatza mi-me'ai mevasheq et-naphshi — David's argument for restraint: if his own son (who came from his own bowels, yatza mi-me'ai, the most intimate biological connection) seeks his life (mevasheq et-naphshi), how much more this Benjaminite! The qal va-chomer (lesser to greater) argument: if the most intimate person wants him dead, the distant enemy's curse is small by comparison.
12: Ulai yir'eh YHWH be-onyi ve-heshhiv YHWH li tovah tachat qillato ha-yom — David's hope: yir'eh YHWH be-onyi (YHWH will look upon my affliction/oni). The noun oni (affliction, humiliation) is the vocabulary of the oppressed petitioner — it appears in Hannah's prayer (1 Samuel 1:11) and the exodus narrative (Exodus 3:7). Ve-heshhiv YHWH li tovah tachat qillato ha-yom — the hope that YHWH will return good for today's curse is the theology of redemptive inversion: curse becoming blessing through divine mediation.
13: Va-yelekh David ve-anashav ba-derekh ve-Shim'i holekh al-tzela ha-har le-negdo — the visual geometry of the parallel march: David on the road, Shimei on the hillside (tzela ha-har, the slope/side of the mountain) moving parallel alongside, cursing and throwing stones and dust (ve-ipper be-aphar). The ipper be-aphar (hurling dust) is an escalating form of contemptuous assault that accompanied stone-throwing.
14: Va-yavo ha-melekh ve-khol-ha-am asher itto ayephim — ayephim (exhausted, weary) from the adjective ayef. The arrival at the Jordan after approximately 30 km of difficult terrain — with women, children, and the entire household — explains the urgency of rest. This exhaustion is precisely the tactical vulnerability that Ahithophel's advice in 2 Samuel 17 correctly identifies: strike now, before David can regroup.
15: Ve-Avshalom ve-khol-ha-am anshei Yisra'el ba'u Yerushalaim ve-Achitophel itto — the arrival of Absalom in Jerusalem with all Israel's men and Ahithophel with him. The weighted final note — Achitophel itto (Ahithophel was with him) — signals the political danger. Ahithophel's presence transforms Absalom's successful coup into a strategically guided threat against David.
16: Va-yehi ka-asher-ba Chushai ha-Arki re'eh David el-Avshalom vayomer Chushai le-Avshalom: yechi ha-melekh yechi ha-melekh — yechi ha-melekh (long live the king) — the standard royal acclamation. The double repetition (yechi yechi) is hyperbolic enthusiasm. The narrator's retention of the title re'eh David (David's Friend) even as Hushai addresses Absalom is a winking signal to the reader: this is still David's Friend performing the role of Absalom's advisor.
17: Vayomer Avshalom el-Chushai: zeh chasedkhah et-re'ekha — Absalom's challenge: chasedkhah (your loyalty, chesed) to your friend. The use of chesed — the covenant-loyalty term — frames Hushai's presence as a violation of the primary loyalty relationship. Absalom is testing whether the friendship with David is genuine or negotiable.
18: Vayomer Chushai el-Avshalom: lo ki asher bachar YHWH ve-ha-am ha-zeh ve-khol ish Yisra'el lo ehyeh ve-itto eshev — Hushai's answer deploys the election vocabulary: bachar YHWH (whom YHWH has chosen). The argument is that YHWH's choice is expressed through the people's choice — and the people have chosen Absalom. The statement is technically true as a statement of political theology while being personally dishonest: Hushai does not believe YHWH has chosen Absalom.
19: Ve-ha-shenit li-mi ani evod ha-lo lifnei vno — “and secondly, whom should I serve? Should it not be before his son?” The dynastic-continuity argument: service to the Davidic line rather than to an individual king. Ka-asher avadti lifnei avikhah ken ehyeh lefanekhah — “as I served before your father, so I will serve before you.” The ambiguity is maintained: technically describing service to the Davidic line, which continues to be true.
20: Vayomer Avshalom el-Achitophel: havu lakhem etza mah na'aseh — Absalom's formal consultation of Ahithophel: havu lakhem etza (give counsel to us). The plural lakhem (to us) includes the assembled elders. Mah na'aseh (what shall we do) is the decision-making formula that activates the counselor's authoritative role. Ahithophel's response will be the most politically acute counsel in the narrative — and the one YHWH will overturn through Hushai.
21: Vayomer Achitophel el-Avshalom: bo el-pilagshei avikhah asher hinnich lishemor ha-bayit — Ahithophel's advice: go to your father's concubines (pilagshei avikhah). Ve-shama kol-Yisra'el ki-niv'ashta et-avikhah — “and all Israel will hear that you have made yourself abhorrent (niv'ashta, Niphal of ba'ash, stink, be loathsome) to your father.” The political logic: an irreversible action forces full commitment from all followers — no one can hedge by hoping for father-son reconciliation. Ve-chazqu yadei kol-asher ittakh — the hands of all with you will be strengthened.
22: Va-yattu le-Avshalom ha-ohel al-ha-gag — the tent (ohel) erected on the rooftop (ha-gag) — the same roof from which David saw Bathsheba (2 Samuel 11:2). Va-yavo Avshalom el-pilagshei aviv le-einei kol-Yisra'el — Absalom went to his father's concubines in the sight of all Israel. Nathan's oracle (2 Samuel 12:11–12) spoke of precisely this public humiliation: ve-natalti et-nashekha le-einekha... ve-shakhav im-nashekha le-einei ha-shemesh ha-zeh (I will take your wives before your eyes... in the sight of this sun). The verbal echo between oracle and fulfillment is exact.
23: Va-atzat Achitophel asher ya'ats ba-yamim ha-hem ke-asher yish'al bi-devar ha-elohim — the narrative assessment of Ahithophel's counsel: in those days his advice was “as though one consulted the word of God.” Ke-asher yish'al bi-devar ha-elohim (as when one inquired of the word of God) — this is the narrator's sociological observation, not theological endorsement: both Absalom's court and David's camp treated his advice as determinative. This exalted reputation is precisely what makes Hushai's counter-counsel so remarkable — and makes its success so explicitly attributed to divine intervention (2 Samuel 17:14).
